title: Tailscale Header Spoofing Allows Gateway Authentication Bypass
description: The gateway's Tailscale authentication mode trusts HTTP headers (`tailscale-user-login`, `x-forwarded-for`, `x-forwarded-proto`, `x-forwarded-host`) solely based on the TCP connection originating from a loopback address (127.0.0.1 or ::1). There is no cryptographic or kernel-level verification that the connection comes from the real Tailscale proxy daemon. Any local process can open a TCP connection to the gateway port and forge these headers, fully bypassing gateway authentication. The `isTailscaleProxyRequest` function at line 97-103 of auth.ts checks only that the remote address is loopback AND the x-forwarded-for/proto/host headers are present — both conditions are trivially satisfied by any local process.
recommendation: Bind the Tailscale-mode gateway to the Tailscale interface address (100.64.0.0/10 range) rather than loopback, and validate that the source IP is in the Tailscale subnet. Alternatively, use Unix domain sockets with file-system permissions, or require the Tailscale daemon to inject a cryptographically signed header. At minimum, document that `tailscale serve` mode is only secure when no other local process can reach the gateway port.
evidence: In auth.ts lines 88-102, `hasTailscaleProxyHeaders` only checks that x-forwarded-for, x-forwarded-proto, and x-forwarded-host headers are non-empty strings. `isTailscaleProxyRequest` accepts any connection from 127.0.0.1/::1 that includes those headers. A loopback TCP client can include arbitrary values for all three headers and `getTailscaleUser` (lines 68-86) will read the forged `tailscale-user-login` header, returning any desired identity to the caller. In `authorizeGatewayConnect` (lines 141-210), when `auth.allowTailscale` is true and auth mode is not 'token' or 'password', the code branches at line 194-207 and grants access based entirely on these forgeable headers.

title: Non-Constant-Time Token Comparison in Gateway WebSocket Auth
description: In `authorizeGatewayConnect` (auth.ts line 174), the gateway token is compared using the `!==` strict equality operator rather than a constant-time comparison function. The `safeEqual` function using `timingSafeEqual` is defined in the same file and is correctly used for password comparison (line 188), but the token comparison at line 174 uses `!==`, making it vulnerable to timing-side-channel attacks. An attacker who can send many connection attempts to the gateway and measure response times can distinguish a correct token prefix from an incorrect one, enabling incremental token brute-forcing.
recommendation: Replace `connectAuth.token !== auth.token` with `!safeEqual(connectAuth.token, auth.token)`. The `safeEqual` function already exists in the same file (lines 34-37) and uses Node's `timingSafeEqual`. Also apply the same fix to the hooks token comparison in server-http.ts line 85.
evidence: At auth.ts line 174, the comparison `connectAuth.token !== auth.token` uses JavaScript's built-in strict equality, which can short-circuit on the first differing character, leaking timing information. The `safeEqual` helper at lines 34-37 uses `Buffer.from` and `timingSafeEqual` and is correctly applied for password comparison at line 188, but inconsistently omitted for token comparison. The identical issue exists in server-http.ts at line 85: `token !== hooksConfig.token`.

title: Non-Constant-Time Token Comparison in HTTP Hooks Endpoint
description: The HTTP hooks endpoint at `server-http.ts` line 85 compares the bearer/header/query token using the `!==` operator, which is not constant-time. This is the same class of vulnerability as in auth.ts and allows timing-based token oracle attacks against the hooks endpoint.
recommendation: Use a constant-time comparison equivalent to the `safeEqual` helper in auth.ts: `import { timingSafeEqual } from 'node:crypto'` and compare `Buffer.from(token)` vs `Buffer.from(hooksConfig.token)` after length-checking.
evidence: The `extractHookToken` function (hooks.ts lines 52-72) collects the token from Authorization header, x-clawdbot-token header, or query string. The token is then compared at server-http.ts line 85 with the raw `!==` operator. This is accessible without any prior authentication, making it a stronger attack target than the WebSocket auth — an attacker can send many GET/POST requests to measure timing.

title: Dynamic import() of User-Controlled Hook Transform Module Path
description: The hook mapping system in `hooks-mapping.ts` loads user-specified JavaScript/TypeScript modules via dynamic `import()` at runtime. While the transform path is configured by the operator in the YAML config file, the path itself is constructed by joining a user-configurable `transformsDir` (hooks.transformsDir) with the mapping's `transform.module` field. A compromised or malicious config entry (or an attacker who gains config write access via the `config.set` WebSocket method) can specify an arbitrary absolute path to any JS file on the local filesystem, causing it to be loaded and executed with full Node.js privileges. The module is also cached in-process permanently (`transformCache`), preventing hot-fix without restart.
recommendation: Validate that the resolved `modulePath` falls within an explicitly configured and sandboxed transforms directory. Reject absolute paths that escape the expected directory. Consider allowlisting transform module names rather than accepting arbitrary paths. At minimum, log a warning when a transform is loaded and enforce that `transformsDir` is within the user's config directory.
evidence: In `normalizeHookMapping` (hooks-mapping.ts line 163-168), the transform path is resolved via `resolvePath(transformsDir, mapping.transform.module)`. The `resolvePath` function (lines 325-329) accepts absolute paths directly (`if (path.isAbsolute(target)) return target`), meaning any operator-set absolute path bypasses the directory prefix entirely. Combined with the fact that any authenticated WebSocket client can call `config.set` to update the hooks configuration, a full chain from authenticated gateway access to arbitrary code execution is possible.

title: macOS Keychain Shell Injection via JSON-Serialized OAuth Credentials
description: In `cli-credentials.ts` at line 280-283, the function `writeClaudeCliKeychainCredentials` constructs a shell command string by embedding a JSON-serialized credential blob into single-quoted shell argument position. The attempt to escape single quotes (`newValue.replace(/'/g, "'\"'\"'")`)) is the correct shell escaping technique for single-quoted strings, but the input `newValue` is the result of `JSON.stringify(existingData)` where `existingData` was read from the keychain and then mutated with OAuth token values (`newCredentials.access`, `newCredentials.refresh`, `newCredentials.expires`). If any of those token values contain single-quote characters (which are valid in JWT tokens that use non-standard encoding), the escaping logic produces a syntactically valid but content-altered string. More critically, the `account` variable in `readCodexKeychainCredentials` (line 102) is interpolated directly into the shell command without any escaping: `` `security find-generic-password -s "Codex Auth" -a "${account}" -w` `` where `account` is derived from a SHA256 hash and is safe in practice, but this pattern is fragile.
recommendation: Replace the shell command string construction with `execFileSync('security', ['add-generic-password', '-U', '-s', CLAUDE_CLI_KEYCHAIN_SERVICE, '-a', CLAUDE_CLI_KEYCHAIN_ACCOUNT, '-w', newValue], ...)`. Using `execFileSync` with an argument array avoids shell interpretation entirely. Apply the same fix to all other `execSync` calls that construct command strings with dynamic values.
evidence: At line 280-283, `execSync` is called with a template literal. The credential value `newValue` is placed inside single quotes with an inline replace for `'` characters. The CLAUDE_CLI_KEYCHAIN_SERVICE constant is `'Claude Code-credentials'` (no special chars), and CLAUDE_CLI_KEYCHAIN_ACCOUNT is `'Claude Code'` (no special chars), so in current practice the static strings are safe. However, `newValue` can contain any characters returned by an OAuth server in access/refresh token values. OAuth tokens using PKCE or custom signing may include characters like `$`, backticks, or other shell metacharacters that survive the single-quote escaping if combined with embedded quotes.

title: Gateway Unauthenticated When Binding to Non-Loopback Addresses in 'none' Auth Mode
description: The gateway enforces at startup (`server.ts` line 541-544) that it will not bind to non-loopback addresses without authentication. However, the check is only `authMode === 'none'`. When `tailscaleMode` is `'serve'` and `allowTailscale` is true, the `resolveGatewayAuth` function at auth.ts line 117-119 computes `allowTailscale = authConfig.allowTailscale ?? (tailscaleMode === 'serve' && mode !== 'password')`. This means with `mode='none'` and `tailscaleMode='serve'`, the gateway IS permitted to bind to loopback with no token/password, relying only on the Tailscale header spoofing vulnerability (THREAT-001) for access control. A user who explicitly sets no auth but enables Tailscale serve may believe they are protected, while in reality any local process can bypass authentication.
recommendation: Add an explicit warning in the gateway startup log when `auth.mode=none` and `allowTailscale=true`, noting that Tailscale header authentication is dependent on process isolation. Document clearly that Tailscale header trust provides no security guarantee for localhost-bound services when other local processes exist. Consider requiring at least a minimal auth token even in Tailscale serve mode to provide defense-in-depth.
evidence: The guard at server.ts line 541-544 only blocks non-loopback binds with auth=none. When tailscaleMode='serve', the gateway binds to loopback (enforced at line 536-540) with auth=none, relying entirely on Tailscale header validation. As documented in THREAT-001, this is spoofable by any local process. The code path is: `resolveGatewayAuth` returns `{mode:'none', allowTailscale:true}`, gateway binds to 127.0.0.1, Tailscale proxy injects headers, but any other local process can also inject those headers.

title: Authenticated Arbitrary Config Write via config.set Enables Persistent Compromise
description: Any authenticated WebSocket client can call `config.set` or `config.apply` (server-methods/config.ts lines 57-111 and 112-210) to fully replace the gateway configuration file with arbitrary content. The only validation is schema validation via `validateConfigObject`. This allows an authenticated attacker to insert malicious hook transform module paths, custom command templates, or enable features like Tailscale Funnel with no-auth mode. Since `config.apply` also triggers a gateway restart (line 192-195), the attacker can atomically persist their config and force reload. This is particularly dangerous because the gateway often runs with auth=token in single-user desktop deployments where token theft (e.g., via a malicious npm package that reads `~/.clawdbot/`) provides a full pivot.
recommendation: This is intentional administrative functionality, but consider adding: (1) audit logging of all config changes with the authenticated user identity, (2) restricting `config.set`/`config.apply` to connections authenticated with password mode rather than token mode, and (3) rate-limiting config write operations. The hook transform module path injection chain (THREAT-003) makes this higher impact than a typical config write.
evidence: In config.ts lines 57-111, `config.set` accepts any JSON5 string that passes schema validation and writes it to disk. In config.ts lines 112-210, `config.apply` additionally schedules a SIGUSR1 restart of the gateway daemon. There is no authorization check beyond the shared gateway authentication — any connected client (token, password, or Tailscale user) has equal config write access. Combined with the dynamic import() vulnerability in hooks-mapping.ts, an attacker who can write config can achieve code execution.

title: Unrestricted Agent Command Execution via Authenticated WebSocket
description: The `agent` handler (server-methods/agent.ts) and the OpenAI-compatible `/v1/chat/completions` HTTP endpoint (openai-http.ts) allow any authenticated client to dispatch arbitrary messages to the AI agent, which may execute bash commands, read/write files, and interact with external services via the bash tool. The `extraSystemPrompt` field accepted by the `agent` handler (lines 65-66) allows injecting additional system prompt content, which can override agent behavior. There is no additional authorization layer between gateway authentication and full agent capabilities.
recommendation: Apply the principle of least privilege: consider adding a separate authorization tier for agent command dispatch (especially extraSystemPrompt injection) vs. read-only operations like health/status checks. At minimum, rate-limit agent invocations per authenticated connection, and consider disabling extraSystemPrompt injection unless explicitly enabled in config.
evidence: In agent.ts lines 294-314, `agentCommand` is called with `extraSystemPrompt: request.extraSystemPrompt` directly from the WebSocket request. The `extraSystemPrompt` field allows the caller to prepend arbitrary instructions to the agent's system prompt, potentially overriding safety or behavior guidelines. The bash tool (bash-tools.ts) can execute arbitrary shell commands. No additional authorization check exists between successful WebSocket auth and full agent + bash command execution.

title: OpenAI-Compat Endpoint Proxies auth.token and auth.password Interchangeably
description: In `openai-http.ts` at lines 186-191, the OpenAI-compatible chat completions endpoint extracts the Bearer token and passes it simultaneously as both `token` and `password` to `authorizeGatewayConnect`. This means the same credential (the OpenAI API key value) works regardless of whether the gateway is configured in 'token' or 'password' auth mode. While functionally convenient, this means a weaker password policy (e.g., shorter passwords) may be used interchangeably with a stronger token, and it creates a confusing security model where the same secret serves dual purposes.
recommendation: Pass the Bearer token only to the auth field that matches the configured gateway auth mode. Check `opts.auth.mode` and pass `{ token }` or `{ password }` accordingly, not both simultaneously.
evidence: In openai-http.ts at lines 186-191, `connectAuth: { token, password: token }` passes the same Bearer token value for both token and password fields. In `authorizeGatewayConnect` (auth.ts), the `token` mode checks `connectAuth.token` (line 172) and the `password` mode checks `connectAuth.password` (line 181). By providing the same value for both, the endpoint effectively bypasses the configured auth mode distinction.
